key_insight: Hydrogen segregation near a straight screw dislocation in bcc iron is strongly controlled by dislocation core structure, site type, and concentration, and can be reduced to a thermodynamic description of the most relevant trapping states.
engineering_relevance: The results provide a multiscale basis for modeling hydrogen embrittlement and plasticity in iron by linking atomistic trapping energetics to simplified continuum-scale interaction laws.
method: Performed systematic molecular simulations with a neural-network interatomic potential.
Explored hydrogen binding across easy-core and hard-core dislocation configurations and multiple trapping sites/concentrations.
Built a thermodynamic framework and assessed when an elastic dipole model remains valid.
evidence_signal: The simulation-based energetics show good agreement with the limited DFT data available in the literature and help rationalize experimental hydrogen solubility limits.
watch_item: The elastic dipole description is only valid within a limited interaction regime, so extrapolation beyond the calibrated core/site/concentration space may be unreliable.
paper_title: Hydrogen segregation around a straight screw dislocation in bcc iron
